title: Files & I/O: Loading Real Data Off Disk
learningObjectives: Read a real CSV file of energy data into a list of dictionaries
Read and parse a real JSON API-style response into Python data structures
Write processed readings back out to a file so results persist between runs
Handle a missing or malformed file gracefully using the error-handling skills from the previous lesson
whyItMatters: Every reading used so far has been hard-coded directly in the script. The AI Energy OS needs to load actual historical demand, weather, and price data from real files — CSV exports from utilities, JSON responses from APIs — and everything built so far (lists, dicts, loops, functions, error handling) exists to process exactly this kind of file.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's actual data-loading layer — reading historical demand/weather CSV exports and JSON API captures from disk into the same structured-record shape used throughout this mission.

problem.summary: Real data lives in files, not Python literals typed into a script.
problem.content: All of your readings so far have been typed directly into the script. A real historical demand dataset lives in a CSV file with thousands of rows, and a weather API response you've saved for testing lives in a JSON file — neither one can be hand-typed as a Python literal.

motivation.summary: This is where the mission stops being simulated entirely inside a script.
motivation.content: This is the moment the mission stops being simulated entirely inside a script and starts touching real, external data. Every later mission — pandas, scikit-learn, PyTorch — assumes you can already get data off disk and into memory reliably.

theory.summary: csv.DictReader and json.load() parse files directly into the shapes already learned.
theory.content: The `csv` module's `csv.DictReader` reads a CSV file row by row, turning each row directly into a dictionary keyed by the header row — the same structured-record shape from the Dictionaries lesson. The `json` module's `json.load()` parses a JSON file directly into nested Python lists/dictionaries. Always open files with `with open(path) as f:` so the file is closed automatically even if an error occurs partway through — and wrap the open/read itself in `try/except` (`FileNotFoundError`, `json.JSONDecodeError`) per the previous lesson, since a missing or corrupt file is exactly the real-world messiness error handling exists for.

visualExplanation.summary: CSV rows flow through DictReader into the same card-shaped records built by hand earlier.
visualExplanation.content: A CSV file's rows flow through `csv.DictReader` and land as a list of dictionary 'cards' identical in shape to the ones built by hand in the Dictionaries lesson — reinforcing that this is the same data shape, just sourced from disk instead of typed inline.

interactiveDemo.summary: Load a sample CSV and JSON file and watch them parse into familiar record cards.
interactiveDemo.content: Select a small sample CSV file and a small sample JSON file and watch them parse live into the same card-based dictionary visualization used in the Dictionaries lesson.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: load a CSV and a JSON file into familiar Python structures.
guidedCoding.content:
```python
import csv
import json

with open("demand_history.csv") as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)
    readings = [row for row in reader]

print(readings[0])

with open("weather_snapshot.json") as f:
    weather = json.load(f)

print(weather["temperature_c"])
```

independentCoding.summary: Load, convert, and re-save a CSV of battery readings, unaided.
independentCoding.content: Given a small CSV file of battery state-of-charge readings, load it with `csv.DictReader`, convert the `soc_percent` field from string to float for every row, and write the classified results to a new CSV file.

debugging.summary: Fix a bug caused by forgetting every CSV value arrives as a string.
debugging.content:
```python
if row["demand_kw"] > 5000:
    print("OVERLOAD")
```
This silently misbehaves because `row["demand_kw"]` is a string (`"5100"`), not a number, and comparing a string to an int doesn't do what you'd expect. Find the missing conversion and fix it.

reflection.summary: Every CSV value arrives as a string, even numeric-looking ones.
reflection.content: Every value read from a CSV file arrives as a string, even if it looks like a number. Where else in this mission would that quietly break something if you didn't already know it?

teachBack.summary: Explain why DictReader producing dictionaries wasn't a coincidence.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone why `csv.DictReader` producing a list of dictionaries wasn't a coincidence, but a direct consequence of everything learned in the Dictionaries lesson.

miniProject.summary: Load the mini project's rolling window from a real CSV file instead of hard-coded values.
miniProject.content: Replace the mini project's hard-coded rolling window with readings actually loaded from a small provided CSV file, keeping the same classification and reporting logic.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This is the actual starting point of the Digital Twin's data layer.
portfolioEvidence.content: This is the first version of the ingestion pipeline that reads real files rather than hard-coded Python literals — the actual starting point of the Digital Twin's data layer.

labs.title: CSV & JSON Loader
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise loading a provided CSV of demand readings and a provided JSON weather snapshot, requiring correct type conversion and a graceful FileNotFoundError path for a deliberately missing file.

simulations.title: File-to-Record Visualizer
simulations.description: Animates CSV rows and JSON objects parsing into the same dictionary 'card' shape used throughout the mission.

flashcards.front: What type is every value read from a CSV file via csv.DictReader, before you convert it?
flashcards.back: A string — even fields that look numeric, like '4821', arrive as the string '4821' and must be explicitly converted (e.g. with float() or int()) before numeric comparisons or math will behave correctly.

flashcards.front: Why use 'with open(path) as f:' instead of f = open(path)?
flashcards.back: The with block guarantees the file is closed automatically once the block ends, even if an error occurs partway through reading — a plain open() without with risks leaving the file handle open on failure.

rubric: Correctly converts every numeric CSV field from string before using it in comparisons or math
File loading is wrapped in error handling per the previous lesson, gracefully reporting a missing or malformed file rather than crashing
Mini project correctly reproduces prior lessons' classification/reporting behavior using data loaded from disk instead of hard-coded values
